### Related literature {#sec-literature}

This paper sits at the intersection of real options, strategic investment, structural credit risk, and the economics of AI. The key novel mechanism is the *training-inference allocation* under regime-switching demand with duopoly competition and endogenous default. I focus here on the direct building blocks; see @dixit1994investment and @smit2004strategic for comprehensive surveys.

The foundation rests on @mcdonald1986value for optimal timing of irreversible investment and @pindyck1988irreversible for capacity choice.^[Other foundational contributions include @brennan1985evaluating, @bernanke1983irreversibility, @majd1987time, @barilan1996investment, and @abeleberly1996optimal on costly reversibility.] @grenadier2002option shows that competition drastically erodes the option value of waiting.^[See also @grenadier1996strategic on development cascades, @back2009open on closed-loop strategies, @weeds2002strategic on R\&D delay, and @fudenberg1985preemption for game-theoretic foundations.] @huisman2015strategic---the closest strategic investment paper---study a duopoly with joint timing and capacity choice. I extend their framework with endogenous default risk, training-inference allocation, diminishing returns, and regime switching. @bouis2009multistage provide the $N$-firm sequential entry structure used in the supplementary numerical extension (@sec-nfirm-appendix).

The default mechanism builds on @merton1974pricing and @leland1994corporate.^[@blackcox1976valuing and @goldstein2001ebit extend to first-passage and dynamic settings.] The most closely related papers combining investment and default are @hackbarth2014capital (real options with endogenous default and product market competition) and @kumar2018optimal (joint leverage and irreversible capacity choice, showing they are substitutes).^[@sundaresan2015dynamic model growth options with default risk; @bolton2011unified and @bolton2019investment model financial constraints; @morellec2004can studies endogenous investment and capital structure; @strebulaev2007tests develops dynamic capital structure with infrequent adjustment; @hennessy2004tobin, @mauer1994interactions, @childs2005interactions, and @miao2005optimal further develop financing-investment interactions.] The present model integrates endogenous default into the strategic capacity-choice game, building on @hackbarth2012corporate for the investment-financing framework.

@guo2005investment model demand with regime-dependent drift and volatility, showing large investment jumps upon favorable regime arrival.^[@bloom2009impact develops time-varying volatility shocks; @bloom2007uncertainty provide firm-level validation; @jovanovic2005general document S-curve diffusion for general-purpose technologies.] AI training exhibits power-law scaling [@kaplan2020scaling; @hoffmann2022training], motivating the concave production function; @jones1995rdbased and @bloom2020ideas study diminishing returns in idea production. On the training-inference allocation, @akcigit2018growth study exploitation versus exploration R\&D---the closest analog, but in a multi-product firm rather than a compute allocation setting.^[@berk1999optimal and @carlson2004corporate develop the growth option decomposition used in @sec-valuation. @katz1986technology and @farrell1986installed study technology adoption with network externalities and installed-base inertia. @babina2024artificial and @eisfeldt2024generative provide empirical context on AI investment and valuations.] On the macroeconomics of AI, @acemoglu2024simple, @korinek2024scenarios, and @jones2024agi analyze growth implications of transformative AI.^[@dellaseta2012optimal combine real options with learning curves; @novymarx2007operating shows that operating leverage amplifies systematic risk, relevant to the operating cost parameter $\delta$.]

The key contribution is placing the training-inference allocation at the center of the investment problem, with regime-specific competition (inference today, training quality tomorrow) and an endogenous regime switch accelerated by firms' aggregate training compute. This mechanism interacts with endogenous default risk and strategic capacity choice, producing a *faith-based survival* mechanism and an *asymmetric AI investment dilemma* in which the training channel amplifies the cost of overinvestment relative to underinvestment.
